full_response:
Let the transformation between the two coordinate systems be

x^{\mu'} = x^{\mu'}(x^0,x^1,x^2,x^3) , μ,μ' = 0,1,2,3.

The curve Γ is described by the parametric equations x^μ = x^μ(λ).  
In the unprimed system the components of the tangent vector are  

v^{\mu} = \frac{dx^{\mu}}{d\lambda}.                (1)

To obtain the components in the primed system, differentiate x^{\mu'}(x^ν(λ)) with respect to λ, applying the chain rule:

v^{\mu'} ≡ \frac{dx^{\mu'}}{d\lambda} 
          = \frac{∂x^{\mu'}}{∂x^{\nu}}\;\frac{dx^{\nu}}{d\lambda}.    (2)

(The index ν is summed from 0 to 3; here and below repeated indices are summed—Einstein summation convention.)

Recognising dx^{\nu}/dλ = v^{\nu} from (1), eq. (2) becomes

v^{\mu'} = \frac{∂x^{\mu'}}{∂x^{\nu}}\;v^{\nu}.                           (3)

If one wishes to display the sum explicitly,

v^{\mu'} = \sum_{\nu=0}^{3}\left( \frac{∂x^{\mu'}}{∂x^{\nu}} \right) v^{\nu}.   (4)

Equation (3)/(4) is the desired transformation law: the components of the tangent vector mix according to the Jacobian matrix ∂x^{\mu'}/∂x^{\nu}, ensuring that the geometric vector vᵃ itself is invariant under the coordinate change.

\boxed{\,v^{\mu'} = \dfrac{∂x^{\mu'}}{∂x^{\nu}}\;v^{\nu} 
      \;=\;
      \sum_{\nu=0}^{3} \left(\dfrac{∂x^{\mu'}}{∂x^{\nu}}\right) v^{\nu}\,}

Confidence: 78%
